title: Sets: Who's Actually Reporting?
learningObjectives: Create a set and explain how it differs from a list (uniqueness, unordered)
Use set operations (union, intersection, difference) to compare groups of sensor sources
Deduplicate a list of readings using a set without writing manual duplicate-checking logic
Recognize when a set is the wrong choice, e.g. when order or duplicate counts matter
whyItMatters: The Digital Twin's data feed reports the same source ('substation-12') on every reading, and you'll eventually need to know things like 'which substations reported today' or 'which sensors are common to both the demand feed and the price feed' — a deduplication and comparison problem sets solve directly, without hand-rolled nested loops.
capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's active-source tracker — knowing which sensors/substations are currently reporting, and comparing coverage between two data feeds (e.g. weather stations vs. demand meters).

problem.summary: Duplicate source names are piling up and you only care about distinct ones.
problem.content: Your growing list of readings each include a `source` field, and duplicates are piling up as the same substation reports every hour. You need to know how many *distinct* substations you've heard from, not how many readings you've received.

motivation.summary: 'How many unique X' and 'what's common to A and B' are recurring questions, not one-off puzzles.
motivation.content: Every optimisation and monitoring feature later needs to answer 'how many unique X' or 'which items are in both A and B.' Writing that by hand with a list and nested loops gets slow and error-prone as data grows; sets answer it directly.

theory.summary: Sets are unordered and unique; union/intersection/difference mirror set theory.
theory.content: A set is an unordered collection of unique values: `sources = {"substation-12", "substation-7"}`. Adding a duplicate has no effect. Set operations mirror mathematical set theory: `a | b` (union), `a & b` (intersection), `a - b` (difference). Converting a list to a set (`set(readings_list)`) is the standard way to deduplicate, but you lose ordering and duplicate-count information in the process.

visualExplanation.summary: A Venn diagram highlights the region each set operation corresponds to.
visualExplanation.content: Overlapping circles (a Venn diagram) represent two sets of substation IDs — one from the demand feed, one from the weather feed — with the intersection and difference regions highlighted live as the corresponding operations run.

interactiveDemo.summary: Drag source ID tags into overlapping circles and run set operations against them.
interactiveDemo.content: Drag substation ID tags into two overlapping circles (a live Venn diagram), then run `&`, `|`, `-` and see exactly which region of the diagram the result corresponds to.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: build sets, deduplicate, and compare two feeds' sources.
guidedCoding.content:
```python
demand_sources = {"substation-12", "substation-7", "substation-12"}
print(demand_sources)  # duplicate silently dropped

weather_sources = {"substation-12", "substation-3"}
print(demand_sources & weather_sources)  # common to both
print(demand_sources - weather_sources)  # only in demand feed
```

independentCoding.summary: Compare two source lists using set operations, unaided.
independentCoding.content: Given two lists of source IDs (one from a battery feed, one from an EV-charger feed), convert both to sets and print which sources report to both feeds and which report only to the EV-charger feed.

debugging.summary: Fix code that tries to index a set like a list.
debugging.content:
```python
sources = {"substation-12", "substation-7"}
print(sources[0])
```
This raises a `TypeError` because sets are unordered and don't support indexing. Explain why the code shouldn't need positional access here at all, and rewrite it to get a needed value correctly.

reflection.summary: Deduplication via set() throws information away.
reflection.content: You just deduplicated readings by converting a list to a set and back. What information did that conversion throw away, and would that matter for a use case like 'how many times did substation-12 report today'?

teachBack.content: Explain to someone why `{"a", "a", "b"}` and `["a", "a", "b"]` give different results when you ask for their length.

miniProject.summary: Track distinct sources across the rolling window.
miniProject.content: Extend the sensor-intake mini project: track the set of distinct sources seen across the rolling window, and print it alongside the existing report.

portfolioEvidence.summary: This becomes the Digital Twin's first coverage-monitoring feature.
portfolioEvidence.content: This source-tracking set becomes the Digital Twin's first coverage-monitoring feature — knowing which sensors are actually reporting.

labs.title: Source Tracker
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise comparing source coverage across two simulated data feeds using set operations, including a deliberate list-indexing-on-a-set bug to fix.

simulations.title: Set Venn Visualizer
simulations.description: Interactive Venn diagram showing union/intersection/difference regions as the learner edits set expressions.

flashcards.front: What happens when you .add() a value to a set that's already in it?
flashcards.back: Nothing changes — sets only ever contain unique values, so adding a duplicate is a no-op.
The intersection — the set of values present in both demand_sources and weather_sources.

rubric: Correctly explains why sets don't support positional indexing, not just that it raises an error
Set-comparison solution uses the correct operation (union/intersection/difference) for each stated question rather than reimplementing it with loops
Correctly identifies what information is lost when deduplicating via set() (order, duplicate counts)
